content.text: The consumption-based model is the pricing and operational foundation of cloud computing. Instead of purchasing capacity (servers, licences, storage) upfront, you pay only for the resources you actually consume — measured per second, per hour, per gigabyte, or per invocation depending on the service type.

This model inverts the traditional IT economics: capital expenditure and long procurement cycles are replaced by a utility-style billing relationship where the meter runs only when resources are active. It is the mechanism that makes cloud agility possible — teams can provision and deprovision infrastructure in minutes with no financial barrier to experimentation.

attrs.alt: Chart comparing flat on-premises cost line vs Azure consumption curve that tracks actual demand, with three pillars: no upfront cost, pay for actual use, scale spend with demand

content.text: Traditional infrastructure requires capital approval before a single server is ordered. Azure resources are available on demand — a developer with a subscription can provision a VM, a database, or a Kubernetes cluster within minutes and receive the first bill 30 days later. This fundamentally changes how organisations approach technology investment:
Zero activation energy for new projects
— no business case needed to experiment; the cost is seen in the bill only if the experiment runs.
— teams do not wait for procurement cycles to start building.
— new product ideas can be tested cheaply; successful ones are scaled; failed ones are deleted with minimal sunk cost.
Billing Granularity: What the Meter Measures
Each Azure service type has its own billing unit. Understanding billing granularity lets you model costs accurately and stop paying for idle resources:

Key rule: inbound data to Azure (ingress) is always free. Outbound data (egress) is billed. This is a design consideration for multi-region and hybrid architectures where data crosses Azure boundaries.
The Meter: How Azure Tracks Consumption

Every Azure resource generates meter records that feed into the monthly invoice. Each meter corresponds to a dimension of usage — a VM generates separate meters for compute hours, managed disk storage, and outbound bandwidth. Understanding which meters a resource generates helps avoid bill surprises:

# List all meters for resources in your subscription (current month)
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date $(date -d 'first day of month' +%Y-%m-01) \
  --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --query '[].{
    Resource:instanceName,
    MeterCategory:meterCategory,
    MeterName:meterDetails.meterName,
    Quantity:usageQuantity,
    Unit:meterDetails.unitOfMeasure,
    Cost:pretaxCost
  }' \
  --output table | sort -k6 -n | tail -20
  # Shows the 20 highest-cost meter records this month

# Drill into a specific resource group
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date $(date -d 'first day of month' +%Y-%m-01) \
  --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --query "[?contains(instanceId,'myapp-rg')].{Meter:meterDetails.meterName, Qty:usageQuantity, Cost:pretaxCost}" \
  --output table
Pay for What You Use: VM Lifecycle Cost Control

The most direct way to reduce VM costs under the consumption model is to deallocate (not just stop) VMs when they are not needed. A stopped (but not deallocated) VM still reserves the compute slot and continues billing. A deallocated VM releases the host and stops all compute charges:

# WRONG: OS-level shutdown still bills for the allocated host
# (equivalent to pressing the power button in Windows/Linux inside the VM)
az vm stop --resource-group myapp-rg --name myapp-vm
# ⚠ Compute charges continue — VM is still on the host

# RIGHT: deallocate releases the host; compute billing stops
az vm deallocate --resource-group myapp-rg --name myapp-vm
# ✓ Compute meter stops immediately
# ✓ Only managed disk and public IP (if static) continue billing

# Automate shutdown/startup on a schedule (saves 65%+ for 9-to-5 workloads)
az vm auto-shutdown \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name myapp-vm \
  --time 1900 \
  --email "team@company.com"
  # Auto-shuts down at 19:00 UTC every day (sends email warning 30 min before)
  # Startup must be triggered manually or via Azure Automation runbook

# Runbook to start a VM at 08:00 each weekday (PowerShell in Azure Automation)
# Start-AzVM -ResourceGroupName 'myapp-rg' -Name 'myapp-vm'

Azure Functions on the Consumption plan realises the consumption model in its purest form — there is no always-on infrastructure at all. Execution capacity is created on demand per invocation and destroyed after the function returns. You pay only for the CPU time and memory used during execution:

# Azure Function: HTTP trigger on Consumption plan
# No idle cost — instances created on demand, destroyed after use
import azure.functions as func
import logging
import time

app = func.FunctionApp(http_auth_level=func.AuthLevel.ANONYMOUS)

@app.route(route="compute")
def compute(req: func.HttpRequest) -> func.HttpResponse:
    start = time.monotonic()
    # Simulate 200ms of work
    result = sum(i * i for i in range(100_000))
    elapsed_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000

    logging.info(f"Computation took {elapsed_ms:.0f}ms")
    return func.HttpResponse(f"Result: {result}, Time: {elapsed_ms:.0f}ms")

# Consumption plan billing for this function:
# - Memory: 256 MB allocated
# - Execution time: ~200ms
# - Cost per invocation: 0.256 GB × 0.2s = 0.0512 GB-seconds × $0.000016 = $0.0000008192
# - 1 million invocations per month: ~$0.82 in compute time + $0.20 execution fee
# - Total: ~$1.02/month for 1M invocations vs ~$30–50/month for always-on App Service
Azure SQL Serverless: Per-Second Compute Billing

Azure SQL Database's serverless compute tier applies consumption billing to a relational database — the database auto-pauses after a configurable idle period (minimum 1 hour) and resumes in seconds on the next connection. You pay for vCore-seconds of compute consumed, not for hours the database exists:

# Create a serverless Azure SQL Database
az sql db create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --server myapp-sql \
  --name myapp-db \
  --compute-model Serverless \
  --edition GeneralPurpose \
  --family Gen5 \
  --min-capacity 0.5 \
  --capacity 4 \
  --auto-pause-delay 60
  # min-capacity: 0.5 vCores when idle (keeps warm connections alive)
  # capacity: max 4 vCores under load
  # auto-pause-delay: pause after 60 minutes of inactivity
  # When paused: only storage is billed (~$0.115/GB/month)
  # When active: billed per vCore-second at ~$0.000145/vCore-second

# Compare: always-on General Purpose 4 vCores
# Always-on: $0.5014/hour × 730 hours/month = $366/month
# Serverless (40% utilisation): ~0.4 × $366 = ~$146/month
# Serverless (10% utilisation / test DB): ~$36/month
Consumption vs Commitment: Choosing the Right Pricing Model

The consumption model is not always the cheapest option. For predictable baseline workloads, committing to capacity in advance (Reserved Instances, Savings Plans) yields significant discounts. The optimal strategy combines both:
Stable compute spend, technology evolution expected
Stable VM SKU, single region workloads

# Practical cost optimisation: identify candidates for Reserved Instances
# A VM running >80% of hours/month is a strong RI candidate

# Query VM uptime from Azure Monitor (hours running per VM last 30 days)
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/prod-rg/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/myapp-vm \
  --metric "VmAvailabilityMetric" \
  --aggregation Average \
  --interval PT1H \
  --start-time $(date -d '30 days ago' +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --end-time $(date +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \
  --query 'value[0].timeseries[0].data | length([?average > `0.5`])' \
  --output tsv
  # Returns count of hours VM was running (> 50% available metric)
  # If > 600 hours (82% of 730 in a month) → strong RI candidate

# Azure Advisor provides RI recommendations automatically:
az advisor recommendation list \
  --category Cost \
  --query "[?shortDescription.solution != null && contains(shortDescription.solution, 'Reserved')] | [0:10].{VM:resourceMetadata.resourceId, Savings:extendedProperties.savingsAmount}" \
  --output table
The Consumption Model and Dev/Test Economics

One of the most immediate benefits of the consumption model is the ability to create full-fidelity development and test environments that run only during working hours. A dev environment that runs 10 hours/day on weekdays uses only 220 hours per month instead of 730 — costing 30% of an always-on equivalent:

# Azure DevTest Labs: enforce auto-shutdown and cost caps for dev environments
az lab create \
  --resource-group devtest-rg \
  --name team-dev-lab

# Set cost threshold: alert at $500, stop new VM creation at $800
az lab cost set \
  --resource-group devtest-rg \
  --lab-name team-dev-lab \
  --target-cost 800 \
  --cost-thresholds '[
    {"thresholdValue": 500, "displayOnChart": true, "sendNotificationWhenExceeded": true},
    {"thresholdValue": 800, "displayOnChart": true, "sendNotificationWhenExceeded": true}
  ]'

# Auto-shutdown all lab VMs at 18:30 daily
az lab policy set \
  --resource-group devtest-rg \
  --lab-name team-dev-lab \
  --policy-set-name default \
  --name LabVmsShutdown \
  --status Enabled \
  --threshold '{"taskType":"LabVmsShutdownTask","dailyRecurrence":{"time":"1830"},"timeZoneId":"UTC"}'

# Economics: Standard_D4s_v5 at $0.192/hour
# Always-on (730h):       $140/month
# Weekday office hours (220h): $42/month
# Weekend excluded, 10h/day: $42/month — saves $98/month per dev VM
Monitoring Consumption to Prevent Bill Shock

The consumption model's pay-as-you-go nature means costs can drift upward if unused resources accumulate. Azure Cost Management provides the visibility and alerting needed to keep consumption-based spend predictable:

# Set up a budget alert at the subscription level
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name "prod-monthly-$$(date +%Y%m)" \
  --amount 10000 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --time-period-start $(date -d 'first day of month' +%Y-%m-01) \
  --time-period-end $(date -d 'first day of next year' +%Y-%m-01) \
  --notifications '[
    {"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":70,"contactEmails":["team@company.com"],"thresholdType":"Actual"},
    {"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":90,"contactEmails":["team@company.com"],"thresholdType":"Actual"},
    {"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":100,"contactEmails":["team@company.com","management@company.com"],"thresholdType":"Actual"},
    {"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":110,"contactEmails":["team@company.com"],"thresholdType":"Forecasted"}
  ]'
  # 70% alert: early warning (7 days left in month typically)
  # 90% alert: action required
  # 100% actual: budget exceeded
  # 110% forecasted: you will exceed budget by month end

# Query daily spend trend to spot anomalies
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date $(date -d '14 days ago' +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --query 'sort_by([].{Date:date, Cost:pretaxCost}, &Date)' \
  --output table
"A company wants to pay only when resources are used" →
"Which pricing model has no upfront cost?" →
Pay-as-you-go (consumption model). Note: Reserved Instances have an optional upfront payment for extra discount.
"What happens to Azure VM billing when the VM is deallocated?" →
Compute charges stop. Storage and static IP charges continue.
The canonical consumption model example: zero cost when idle, billed per invocation and per GB-second of execution.
The exam distinguishes consumption-based (pay per use) from fixed pricing (Reserved Instances). Both are cloud options; they are not mutually exclusive.

Azure Blob Storage extends the consumption model with access tiers — different price points for data based on how frequently it is accessed. Data automatically moves between tiers (Lifecycle Management) so that costs track actual access patterns rather than the worst-case assumption of always-hot storage:

The Archive tier is approximately 18× cheaper than Hot per GB stored but requires hours to rehydrate data before it is readable. The lifecycle management policy automates tier transitions based on last-modified date:

Cost example: 100 TB of log data. Hot tier: $1,800/month. Archive tier: $99/month. Lifecycle policy applied over 6 months saves approximately $1,700/month — the most impactful single optimisation for high-volume log storage.

Networking is often the largest surprise in first Azure bills because the cost model differs significantly from compute and storage. Understanding the egress billing rules prevents unexpected charges:
is always free — uploading data, syncing backups to Azure, or receiving traffic from the internet does not incur transfer charges.
Outbound (egress) data from Azure to the internet
is billed: first 5 GB/month free, then ~$0.087/GB for Zone 1 (Americas, Europe). First 100 GB/month discounted for eligible services.
is billed at lower peering rates (~$0.02/GB for same-continent transfers). Data between regions in different continents is billed at higher rates.
Traffic within the same Azure region
is free — VMs in the same region communicating via private IPs incur no bandwidth charges.
— private circuit traffic is cheaper than internet egress for high-volume hybrid scenarios.

# Identify which resources are generating the most egress spend
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date $(date -d 'first day of month' +%Y-%m-01) \
  --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --query "[?meterCategory=='Bandwidth' || meterCategory=='Virtual Network'].{Resource:instanceName, Meter:meterDetails.meterName, GB:usageQuantity, Cost:pretaxCost}" \
  --output table | sort -k4 -n | tail -20

# Reduce egress costs: use Azure CDN to cache responses at edge PoPs
# Clients hit the CDN PoP (free within Azure CDN); CDN origin pull is cheap
az cdn profile create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name myapp-cdn \
  --sku Standard_Microsoft

az cdn endpoint create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --profile-name myapp-cdn \
  --name myapp-cdn-endpoint \
  --origin myapp.azurewebsites.net \
  --origin-host-header myapp.azurewebsites.net
  # CDN caches responses at 200+ PoPs globally;
  # reduces origin egress charges by serving cached content locally

The consumption model enables accurate cost modelling before any resource is deployed. The Azure Pricing Calculator and Azure Cost Estimator let teams build monthly cost estimates by selecting service types, regions, SKUs, and expected usage volumes. This shifts cost conversation to design time rather than post-deployment:

# Azure Pricing Calculator: web UI at https://azure.microsoft.com/pricing/calculator/
# Programmatic cost estimation via Retail Prices API (no authentication required)

# Get current retail price for Standard_D4s_v5 in East US (pay-as-you-go)
curl -s "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices?\$filter=serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' and armSkuName eq 'Standard_D4s_v5' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' and priceType eq 'Consumption' and contains(productName,'Windows') eq false" \
  | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
for item in data['Items']:
    if 'Spot' not in item.get('skuName','') and 'Low Priority' not in item.get('skuName',''):
        print(f"SKU: {item['skuName']}")
        print(f"Price: \${item['retailPrice']:.4f}/{item['unitOfMeasure']}")
        print(f"Monthly (730h): \${item['retailPrice']*730:.2f}")
        break
"
# Output example:
# SKU: D4s v5
# Price: $0.1920/1 Hour
# Monthly (730h): $140.16
What-If Scenarios with the TCO Calculator

Before committing to cloud migration, the Azure TCO Calculator quantifies the full on-premises cost including often-overlooked categories. A typical 3-year TCO analysis for a 50-server on-premises environment reveals:
Consumption Model for AI and ML Workloads

The consumption model is particularly valuable for AI and machine learning workloads, which have highly variable compute requirements — model training requires powerful GPU VMs for hours or days; inference may be sporadic. Azure offers consumption-based AI services that eliminate the need to own GPU infrastructure:

# Azure OpenAI Service: consumption billing (tokens processed)
# No GPU reservation required; pay per token

import openai
import os

client = openai.AzureOpenAI(
    azure_endpoint=os.environ["AZURE_OPENAI_ENDPOINT"],
    api_key=os.environ["AZURE_OPENAI_KEY"],
    api_version="2024-02-01"
)

response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model="gpt-4o",
    messages=[
        {"role": "system", "content": "You are a helpful assistant."},
        {"role": "user", "content": "Explain the Azure consumption model in one paragraph."}
    ]
)

print(response.choices[0].message.content)

# Billing: based on tokens consumed (input + output)
# GPT-4o: ~$0.005 per 1K input tokens, ~$0.015 per 1K output tokens
# A typical chat response (500 input + 300 output tokens):
#   500/1000 × $0.005 + 300/1000 × $0.015 = $0.0025 + $0.0045 = $0.007 per call
# 100,000 calls/month: $700 — versus owning GPU cluster: $10,000+/month

# Azure Machine Learning: compute cluster that scales to 0 when idle
az ml compute create \
  --resource-group ml-rg \
  --workspace-name myml-workspace \
  --name gpu-cluster \
  --type AmlCompute \
  --min-instances 0 \
  --max-instances 4 \
  --size Standard_NC6s_v3 \
  --idle-time-before-scale-down 120
  # min-instances 0: cluster scales to 0 when no jobs are running
  # Standard_NC6s_v3: 6 vCPU, 112 GB RAM, 1× V100 GPU: ~$3.06/hour
  # idle-time-before-scale-down: 2 minutes after last job completes
  # Cost: only pay during actual training runs, not 24/7 GPU reservation
Since egress is billed under the consumption model, architectural choices that reduce data leaving Azure directly reduce costs. Three effective patterns:
— rather than exporting data to on-premises systems for analysis, run analytics in Azure where data transfer is free within the region. Azure Synapse Analytics and Databricks process data in place.

— traffic between Azure services over Private Endpoints stays on the Microsoft backbone and avoids internet egress billing. A storage account with a private endpoint serves VMs in the same region at no bandwidth cost.
— cache static and dynamic responses at CDN PoPs. Client requests are served from the nearest PoP rather than your origin, reducing both latency and origin egress charges.

# Create a Private Endpoint for a Storage Account (eliminates egress billing for VMs in same region)
az network private-endpoint create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name storage-private-endpoint \
  --vnet-name myapp-vnet \
  --subnet private-endpoints \
  --private-connection-resource-id \
    /subscriptions/$SUB/resourceGroups/myapp-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mystorage \
  --group-id blob \
  --connection-name mystorage-connection

# Register private DNS so VMs resolve mystorage.blob.core.windows.net to private IP
az network private-dns zone create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --name "privatelink.blob.core.windows.net"

az network private-dns link vnet create \
  --resource-group myapp-rg \
  --zone-name "privatelink.blob.core.windows.net" \
  --name myapp-dns-link \
  --virtual-network myapp-vnet \
  --registration-enabled false
  # After this: VMs in myapp-vnet access storage at private IP — no egress billing
= pay for what you use, when you use it. No upfront cost, no idle capacity waste.
varies by service: VMs bill per second (when running); storage bills per GB/month; Functions bill per invocation + execution time.
— deallocate (not just stop) to halt compute billing. Scheduled auto-shutdown saves 65%+ for 9-to-5 dev environments.
Serverless (Functions, SQL Serverless, Cosmos DB autoscale)
takes the consumption model to its logical extreme — zero idle cost, scale from zero on demand.
— pay-as-you-go for variable workloads; Reserved Instances or Savings Plans for predictable baseline capacity (up to 72% discount).
Cost Management budgets and alerts are the essential governance layer that keeps consumption-based spending predictable.
